coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-modified mesoporous SnO2 hierarchical hollow cubes for low temperature detection of nitrogen dioxide

Li, Yan-Yang
Yao, Hong-Chang
Li, Zhong-Jun

Mesoporous SnO2
NO2 sensing
Low temperature
Heterojunctions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis SnS2 and SnO2 hierarchical hollow cube
Synthesis of N-GQDs modified SnO2 composites
Construction for sensors and gas sensing measurement

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Scientific Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of SnO2 and NG/Inx samples.
SEM images of (a, b) SnS2 and (c, d) NG/Sn1.5 .
TEM images of (a) N-GQDs, (b, c) SnS2, and (d, e) NG/Sn1.5 . (f) HRTEM image and (g) EDS element mapping of NG/Sn1.5 .
(a) Sn 3d, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s XPS spectra of NG/Sn1.5 .
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) pore-size distribution plots of SnS2, SnO2, and NG/Sn1.5 .

(a) Responses of SnO2 and NG/Snx at different working temperatures for 1 ppm NO2. (b) Response curve of NG/Sn1.5 to 1 ppm NO2 at 130 °C. (c) Variation of Ra of SnO2 and NG/Snx with working temperature. (d) The dynamic resistance curves of SnO2 and NG/Snx to NO2 (inset shows the Ra). (e) The response values of NG/Sn1.5 under different NO2 concentration. (f) Response curves of NG/Sn1.5 sensor to 1 ppm NO2 at 20 % ∼ 70 % RH.
(a) The response of NG/Sn1.5 to 1 ppm NO2 in 45 days. (b) The dynamic resistance curves of NG/Sn1.5 to 1 ppm NO2 after 45 days.
Diagram of the energy band structure (a) before and (b) after SnO2 and N-GQDs form a heterojunction. (c) The schematic diagram of the NG/Sn1.5 sensor exposed to air and NO2.
PL spectra of SnO2 and NG/Sn1.5 .
Synthetic schematic diagram of N-GQDs modified SnO2 hierarchical hollow cube for gas sensing.
Comparison of the NG/Sn1.5 sensor in this work and some typical SnO2-based NO2 sensors.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-modified mesoporous SnO2 hierarchical hollow cubes for low temperature detection of nitrogen dioxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Catalysis Center, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Green Catalysis Center, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is an important industrial gas for producing fertilizers and explosives [1,2]. However, NO2 is also regarded as a toxic air pollutant, causing a series of respiratory diseases [3–6]. The U. S. Environment Protection Agency (USEPA) announced that the corneal nerve and respiration system of humans and animal beings will be damaged while exposed to NO2 at concentrations greater than 1 ppm [7–9]. In addition, atmospheric NO2 derived from industrial and vehicle exhaust gas can cause acid rain and photochemical smog [10,11]. Therefore, it is of great essential to develop high-performance gas sensors that can quickly and accurately detect low concentration NO2.

In recent decades, various types of gas sensors including optical, electrochemical, and chemiresistive type of metal oxide semiconductor (MOS) have been widely researched to monitor NO2 concentrations [8,9]. Among them, chemiresistive type of MOS-based gas sensors attracted much attention of researchers due to their unique advantages of high precision, low power consumption, abundant raw materials, and low cost [12]. So far, various MOS such as WO3, CuO, and SnO2 have been used to prepare NO2 gas sensors [13–16]. In the study of these about MOS gas sensing materials, SnO2, an n-type semiconductor, has been regarded as one of the most promising sensing materials for detecting NO2 [17,18]. However, the high working temperature, low response to low-concentration gas, and the agglomeration in the synthesis process hinder its wider application [18]. In order to further meet the demand for high-performance gas sensors, SnO2 materials with various morphologies, including nanoflowers, nanosheets, and hollow structures, have been designed to improve their sensing performance [13,19–21]. It is worth mentioning that the three-dimensional (3D) hollow structure has attracted wide attention of scientists due to its excellent gas diffusion channel and large specific surface area. For example, Zhang et al. prepared SnO2 hollow microspheres with high NO2 sensing performance through carbon nanospheres as templates [20]. Cho et al. synthesized hollow SnO2 nanofibers via electrospinning technology, and their NO2 response was significantly improved compared with planar SnO2 film [21]. The enhanced NO2 sensing property is attributed to larger active areas and greater space charge modulation depth in hollow structures [21].

Apart from morphology control, constructing heterojunction is another simple way to increase the sensitivity of SnO2 gas sensors, because the formation of heterojunction is conducive to expanding the depletion zone and separating electron-hole pairs [12]. In particular, loading applicable material to the SnO2 surface evenly and tightly is the key to effectively improving the gas sensing performance [22]. Among various alternative materials for constructing heterojunctions, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have extraordinary application potential due to the large specific surface area, quantum size, and unique electronic properties [23,24]. By constructing 0D/3D N-GQDs/SnO2 heterojunctions, the agglomeration problem in the composite process can be avoided, and the number of effective heterojunction interfaces can be greatly increased. Moreover, the more negative adsorption energy of N-doped graphene-based materials for NO2 is expected to improve the selectivity of composite materials, and is beneficial to NO2 detection at low temperatures [25–29].

All chemicals purchased were analytical grade reagents and were used without further purification. graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from graphite flake according to the modified Hummers’ method [30]. N-GQDs [31] and ZnSn(OH)6 hollow cube [32] were prepared according to the method reported in the literature. N-GQDs modified SnO2 gas sensor was prepared with ZnSn(OH)6 as precursor and the synthesis process is shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis SnS2 and SnO2 hierarchical hollow cube
ce:section.ce:para: The SnS2 hierarchical cube is prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method [33]. Briefly, 40 mL of deionized water, 57.2 mg of ZnSn(OH)6 hollow cube, 160 mg of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, and 90 mg of thioacetamide were added into a autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 220 °C for 3 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the yellow SnS2 precipitate was washed with ethanol three times and dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight. To obtain SnO2 hierarchical hollow cubes, the as-prepared SnS2 hierarchical hollow cubes were calcined in the muffle furnace at 400 °C for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-GQDs modified SnO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of the N-GQDs modified SnO2 composites, 250 μL N-GQDs suspension (1 mg mL−1) and 50 mg SnO2 were dispersed in 5 mL of water under vigorous stirring. Then, the above suspension was vacuum dried and heated in a tube furnace at 250 °C for 2 h. Similarly, 0.5, 1.5, and 2.5 wt% N-GQDs modified SnO2 composites were synthesized by the procedure above, respectively. The corresponding materials are named as NG/Snx (x = 0.5, 1.5, and 2.5), which represents the theoretical proportion of N-GQDs is 0.5, 1.5, and 2.5 wt%, respectively.

The crystal structures of as-prepared samples were examined by Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD; PANalytical X'pert PRO) with Cu Kα1 (λ =1.5406 Å) radiation source. The morphology and phase composition of as-prepared samples were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss SIGMA 500) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20-S-TWIN). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PerkinElmer PHI 5300) was adopted to examine the surface composition and chemical state of the samples. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR, Nicolet Nexus 470) and the ultraviolet − visible (UV − vis, TU-1810PC) absorption spectrum were carried out to study chemical bonds of N-GQDs. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore-size distribution were measured with a gas absorption analyzer (NOVA, 77 K). The Raman spectra were obtained on a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the SnO2 and NG/Sn1.5 were obtained through a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction for sensors and gas sensing measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The gas sensors were fabricated as follows: the as-prepared samples were mixed with a few drops of isopropyl alcohol and adjusted to a paste. Then, the paste was coated on an alumina tube with a pair of Au electrodes and dried at 80 °C. Four platinum wires were connected to two gold electrodes for conduction. A Ni-Cr heating wire was inserted into the alumina tube to control the heating temperature.

The gas sensing properties of the sensors were measured by a WS-30A gas sensor test system (Wei Sheng Electronics Technology Co., China) at a relative humidity (RH) of 20–25 %. The sensor response was recorded by the change of resistance under a direct voltage of 5 V. The sensor response to oxidizing gases (reducing gases) are defined as R g/R a (R a/R g), where R g and R a are the resistance in the tested gases and air, respectively. Response time and recovery time are respectively defined as the time when the sensor resistance change reaches 90 % after the test gas is injected or discharged. The gas-sensing test was carried out in the static test system. Briefly, the gas-sensing test was performed in a closed air chamber. NO2, HCHO, NH3, CH4, and CO gases (Air products; Balanced with 99.99 % pure air) were injected into the reaction chamber with a microsyringe. The benzene and methanol liquids are injected into the micro heating plate in the reaction chamber with a microinjector to volatilize for detection. In the selectivity evaluation, the test environment of all interfering gases is the same as that of NO2.

The crystal structure and purity of ZnSn(OH)6, SnS2, SnO2, and NG/Snx were determined by XRD. The results demonstrate that ZnSn(OH)6 (JCPDs. 20–1455), hexagonal SnS2 (JCPDs. 101–1010), and rutile SnO2 (JCPDs. 41–1445) were successfully synthesized without additional impurities (Fig. 1 and S1). In addition, the crystallinity of SnO2 remains perfect after modification of N-GQDs. However, the diffraction peaks of N-GQDs do not appear in NG/Snx composites due to the weak intensity of N-GQDs with low content.

The morphology of SnS2, ZnSn(OH)6, and NG/Sn1.5 were observed by SEM. It is clear that the configuration of ZnSn(OH)6 are hollow cubes with an edge length of about 2.3 μm (Fig. S2). After hydrothermal sulfidation, the SnS2 hierarchical hollow cubes (Fig. 2 a and b) assembled from random nanosheets with an average thickness of 13.6 nm (Fig. S3a) were obtained. For the NG/Sn1.5 , it can still maintain a good hierarchical hollow structure after calcination and N-GQDs modification (Fig. 2c and d). In addition, the thickness of SnO2 nanosheets is reduced to about 9.5 nm (Fig. S3b) compared to SnS2 nanosheets, and a large number of mesopores are generated on the SnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 2d).

The morphology and phase composition of the N-GQDs, ZnSn(OH)6, SnS2, and NG/Sn1.5 were further observed by TEM. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a that quantum dots with a lateral size of about 2.7 nm have been successfully prepared. In addition, FT-IR and UV–vis absorption spectra confirmed that N atoms were successfully doped in quantum dots (Fig. S4) [34,35]. The hollow structure of ZnSn(OH)6 (Fig. S5), as well as the well-defined hierarchical hollow structure of the SnS2 (Fig. 3b) and NG/Sn1.5 (Fig. 3d) are also further confirmed by TEM. The TEM image clearly shows that the surface of the SnS2 nanosheets (Fig. 3c) is relatively smooth, whereas after calcination, each nanosheet is composed of a large number of SnO2 nanocrystals (Fig. 3e). The high-magnification (Fig. 3e) and high-resolution (Fig. 3f) TEM images of NG/Sn1.5 indicate that N-GQDs are well decorated on the surface of mesoporous SnO2 nanosheets. In addition, N, C, O, and Sn are evenly distributed in the same area in the EDS mapping, proving the formation of NG/Sn1.5 heterojunction.

The surface chemical compositions and chemical states of as-prepared NG/Sn1.5 were studied by XPS. The XPS spectra of NG/Sn1.5 indicate the presence of N, O, C, and Sn in the composite (Fig. S6). Fig. 4 a shows that Sn 3d spectrum of NG/Sn1.5 included two peaks at 495.1 and 486.7 eV, which are attributed to Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2, respectively [17]. Fig. 4b shows the C 1s peaks of NG/Sn1.5 composites, the four fitted peaks at about 288.8, 286.2, 285.4, and 284.6 eV can be ascribed to the CO, CO, CN, and CC/C=C, respectively. The N 1s spectrum of NG/Sn1.5 (Fig. 4c) indicates the presence of graphitic N (400.7), pyrrolic N (400.0 eV), and pyridinic N (399.8 eV) atoms. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) is fitted by three sub-spectra components at 532.8, 531.8, and 530.7 eV, corresponding to CO, oxygen vacancies (OV), and lattice oxygen (OL), respectively. Furthermore, the unique D band (1349 cm−1) and G band (1591 cm−1) of carbon materials appear in the Raman spectrum of NG/Sn1.5 , further indicating the formation of N-GQDs/SnO2 heterojunction (Fig. S7) [36].

Fig. 5 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distribution of SnS2, SnO2, and NG/Sn1.5 hierarchical hollow cubes. The isotherms of all the samples (Fig. 5a) belong to type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicating the nature of the mesoporous structure [37]. In addition, compared to SnS2, SnO2 and NG/Sn1.5 have abundant mesopores at around 8 nm (Fig. 5b), which corresponds to the SEM results. Abundant mesopores are conducive to gas diffusion and increase of adsorption active sites, which are helpful for improving the gas sensing properties.

It is well known that the gas sensing performance of semiconductor gas sensors is closely dependent on the working temperature. Therefore, the responses of SnO2 and NG/Snx to NO2 gas (1 ppm) at different working temperatures (80−160 °C) were recorded, and the corresponding response and resistance change curves are shown in Fig. 6 a and S8-S11, respectively. The results show that the response of NG/Snx increases first and then decreases with the increase of working temperature, reaching the maximum value at 130 °C. In addition, it can be seen that the content of N-GQDs plays a key role in NO2 response. When the mass ratio of N-GQDs increases from 0.5 wt% (NG/Sn0.5 ) to 1.5 wt% (NG/Sn1.5 ), the NO2 response value increases rapidly and reaches the maximum. However, the response becomes worse as the mass ratio of N-GQDs further increases. Similar phenomena have been reported in other graphene/semiconductor composites [38]. It is worth noting that compared to SnO2, the optimal working temperature of NG/Snx is reduced, and NG/Sn1.5 has the highest response (Rg/Ra = 417) to 1 ppm NO2 at 130 °C. Besides, the evaluation of response/recovery speed indicates that the response/recovery time of NG/Sn1.5 to 1 ppm NO2 gradually decreases with increasing working temperature, and the response and recovery times are 59 s and 33 s at 130 °C, respectively (Fig. 6b and S12). The quick response/recovery speed of the NG/Sn1.5 sensing may be attributed to the special morphology assembled from ultrathin mesoporous nanosheets, which can offer good gas permeability and sufficient active sites rapid gas diffusion and absorption-desorption.

Fig. 6c shows the Ra values of all sensors at different working temperatures. The Ra value of all sensors decreases with the increase of working temperature, which is related to the nature of the semiconductor sensing layer [39]. Additionally, it can be seen from Fig. 6c that the Ra increases with the increase of the modification amount of N-GQDs. This may be attributable to the construction of heterojunctions leading to the formation of a space charge layer at the interface between N-GQDs and SnO2, thereby increasing the resistance of the composite material [39]. Therefore, the Ra of the sensing material increases with the increase of the N-GQDs content on the surface of SnO2.

Fig. 6d and S13 respectively show the dynamic resistance curves and response curves of SnO2 and NG/Snx sensors to different concentrations of NO2 at 130 °C. The results show that all sensors shows excellent response and recovery ability during continuous detection of different concentrations of NO2 (Fig. 6d). The responses of NG/Snx sensors to NO2 are higher than that of SnO2, and NG/Sn1.5 shows the highest response in whole processes (Fig. S12), indicating that the as-prepared sensor can detect NO2 in the air in real-time and quickly. Notably, the response of NG/Sn1.5 is almost linear with the concentration of NO2 (Fig. 6e), and it can effectively detect NO2 as low as 100 ppb (Rg/Ra = 25.3), which is far below the threshold announced by the USEPA.

In the actual application environment, the RH poses a ubiquitous effect on the sensor performance. Therefore, the response of the NG/Sn1.5 sensor to NO2 at different RH was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6f, the response of NG/Sn1.5 to NO2 gradually decreases with the increase of RH. The decrease in response with RH is attributed to the fact that water molecules occupy part of the NO2 adsorption active sites. Additionally, increased water molecule will consume more NO2 because NO2 is dissolvable in water [29]. However, the response of NG/Sn1.5 to 1 ppm can still reach 201 even at 70 % RH, indicating that the sensor can effectively detect NO2 concentration at high RH. In order to further clarify the advantages of the NG/Sn1.5 sensor, Table 1 lists the comparison of the typical SnO2-based gas sensor reported before and the NG/Sn1.5 sensor in this work for NO2 gas sensing property. As can be seen, compared to previous reports, NG/Sn1.5 has a relatively low working temperature, higher response, and faster response/recovery speed.

The gas-sensing test of NG/Sn1.5 to various gases, including CH4, HCHO, NH3, CO, benzene, and methanol, were conducted to examine its gas selectivity. Clearly, the response value of NG/Sn1.5 sensor to 1 ppm NO2 is 417, while the response to other high-concentration interference gases (50 ppm) is less than 2, as shown in Fig. 7 . These results indicate that the NG/Sn1.5 sensor has impressive selectivity to NO2 gas. In addition to selectivity, long-term stability is also a key factor in practical applications. Therefore, the response of the NG/Sn1.5 sensor to 1 ppm NO2 was monitored for 45 days (Fig. 8 a). Obviously, the response of the NG/Sn1.5 sensor has no appreciable degradation even after 45 days. More importantly, the sensor after 45 days was repeatedly exposed to 1 ppm NO2 gas, and still maintained a good response/recovery ability with no signs of attenuation (Fig. 8b).

The sensing mechanism of SnO2-based gas sensors for NO2 has been well explained in previous studies [41,48–50]. For a pure SnO2 sensor, when the SnO2 material is exposed to the air, the electrons in its conduction band (CB) are captured by adsorbed oxygen molecules to form O− (100−300 °C) [51], and an electron depletion layer is generated [49,50]. When NO2 gas is introduced into the detection environment, NO2 molecules can further capture electrons in the CB of SnO2 to form NO2 −, and react with O− on the surface (eq. 1 and 2) [52,53]. This process widens the thickness of the electron depletion layer, which bring about an increase in the resistance of the sensing material, and shows n-type sensing characteristics [50].

For NG/Snx , highly enhanced NO2 sensing behavior is mainly due to the following reasons. First, the formation of heterojunctions between N-GQDs and SnO2 plays a vital role in enhancing NO2 response. The work functions of N-GQDs and SnO2 are approximately 5.0 and 4.5 eV, respectively, according to literature reports [29,41]. Therefore, when N-GQDs contact SnO2, electrons will migrate from SnO2 to N-GQDs until the Fermi level is balanced, as shown in Fig. 9 a and b. The construction of NG/Snx heterojunction leads to the energy band bending at the heterojunction interface and the formation of an additional interface depletion layer [54]. Furthermore, the PL spectra indicate that the formation of NG/Snx heterojunctions can enhance the separation efficiency of carriers, which is more conducive to the NO2 molecules trapping electrons on the surface of the material (Fig. 10 ) [55]. As a result, when NG/Snx is exposed to NO2 atmosphere, NO2 molecules can quickly capture electrons in the CB of SnO2 and N-GQDs, resulting in a larger potential barrier height and a greatly increased depletion layer at the heterojunction interface (Fig. 9c) [54]. Therefore, NG/Snx has a more abrupt resistance change compared to pure SnO2, and thus a higher NO2 response is achieved.
(1) NO2 (gas) + e− → NO2 − (ads)
(2) NO2 (gas) + O− → NO3 − (ads)

Second, compared to semiconductors, N-doped graphene-based materials have a stronger interaction with NO2 due to more negative adsorption energy [28,56]. In particular, doped N atoms with high electron density can provide more adsorption active sites for NO2 with strong electrophilic ability [26,28,29]. Moreover, the higher electronic partial density of states caused by N doping is beneficial to the electron transfer in the NO2 sensing process [57]. Therefore, modification of N-GQDs on SnO2 can effectively increase the adsorption of NO2, thereby improving the response of the composite material and lowering the working temperature.

Finally, the unique hierarchical hollow cube structure and a large number of mesopores on the SnO2 nanosheets provide favorable adsorption and diffusion channels, enabling rapid adsorption and desorption of NO2. Also, abundant mesopores and nanograin boundaries provide more depletion layers required in the sensing process, bring about increased resistance changes after exposure to NO2 gas [15].

In conclusion, the novel mesoporous SnO2 hierarchical hollow cubes modified by N-GQDs were successfully synthesized by the sulfuration-oxidation method followed by the impregnation-calcination method. The sulfuration-oxidation method can not only prepare a uniform hierarchical hollow cubic structure composed of ultrathin SnO2 nanosheets, but also produce a large number of mesopores on the nanosheets. The novel mesoporous hierarchical structure is beneficial to the improvement of NO2 sensing performance due to its stable gas adsorption and diffusion channels, large specific surface area, and abundant nanoscale grain boundaries. In addition, the construction of 0D/3D NG/Snx heterojunction can greatly increase the space charge modulation depth and NO2 adsorption active sites of the composite material, thereby improving its NO2 sensing performance. As a result, the optimal NG/Snx sensor shows fast response/recovery speed (59/33 s) and high response value (Rg/Ra = 417) to 1 ppm NO2. Moreover, the optimal NG/Snx sensor has low working temperature (130 °C), excellent repeatability, long-term stability, and charming selectivity for NO2 gas. More importantly, the optimal NG/Snx sensor shows outstanding detection ability (Rg/Ra = 25.3) for low concentration NO2 (100 ppb). In summary, the research of NG/Snx gas sensor can provide new ideas for preparing high-performance low-temperature NO2 gas sensor.

This work was conceived by Yan-Yang Li and Hong-Chang Yao. Ya-Kun Lv performed the experiments and collected data. Zhong-Jun Li contributed intellectually to the analysis and interpretation of the data. Ya-Kun Lv wrote the original manuscript; Yan-Yang Li, Hong-Chang Yao Zhong-Jun Li revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the financial supports of National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 21471133 and 21671176) and Key scientific research projects of colleges and universities in Henan Province (no. 20A150041)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129882.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene sheets by a combined chemical and hydrothermal reduction of graphene oxide
Langmuir

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ya-Kun Lv received his Master degree from the College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University (Zhengzhou, China) in 2020. He is working for his PhD in the College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His primary works is the synthesis and applications of semiconducting metal oxide materials.

Yan-yang Li received his PhD from College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. Currently, he works in the College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His current research interests focus on the synthesis and application of gas sensing materials and photocatalytic materials.

Hong-Chang Yao is a professor of College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. He received his PhD in inorganic chemistry from Nanjing University with Professor Li-Min Zheng. His current research interests focus on the synthesis and application of gas sensing materials and photocatalytic materials.

Zhong-Jun Li received his PhD from Central South University, China. Currently, he is a professor of inorganic chemistry at Zhengzhou University where he leads a research group dedicated to the synthesis and characterization of functional materials, especially gas sensing materials, catalytic materials and luminescent materials.
